data.content:
Here are the three evaluation performance issues in this project:

---

### 1. Deep Import Chain (level1 → level2 → level3)

The `.csproj` imports `level1.props`, which imports `level2.props`, which imports `level3.props`. Each `<Import>` adds to the evaluation phase — MSBuild must locate, parse, and evaluate each file before any build targets run. Deep chains (typically >20 levels) become significant, and they make the project harder to reason about. Consider flattening into a single `.props` file.

### 2. Overly Broad Glob Pattern

```xml
<AdditionalFiles Include="**\*.*" Exclude="**\bin\**;**\obj\**" />
```

The `**\*.*` glob walks the **entire directory tree** during evaluation. This will scan into `.git/`, `node_modules/`, and any other large directories. This happens at evaluation time — before any build work begins — and can drastically slow things down.

**Fix:** Restrict the glob to specific extensions or directories, and add large directories to `DefaultItemExcludes`:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <DefaultItemExcludes>$(DefaultItemExcludes);node_modules\**;.git\**</DefaultItemExcludes>
</PropertyGroup>
<AdditionalFiles Include="**\*.cs;**\*.json" />
```

### 3. File I/O Property Function During Evaluation

```xml
<BuildNotes>$([System.IO.File]::ReadAllText('build-notes.txt'))</BuildNotes>
```

This calls `System.IO.File.ReadAllText()` during the **evaluation phase** — the phase where MSBuild resolves all properties and items. File I/O here blocks evaluation and runs every time the project is evaluated (IDE loads, design-time builds, etc.). If the file were large or on a slow disk, this would be very costly.

**Fix:** Move file reading to a target in the **execution phase** so it only runs when actually needed:

```xml
<Target Name="ReadBuildNotes" BeforeTargets="Build">
  <ReadLinesFromFile File="build-notes.txt" Condition="Exists('build-notes.txt')">
    <Output TaskParameter="Lines" PropertyName="BuildNotes" />
  </ReadLinesFromFile>
</Target>
```

---

### Evaluation vs. Execution

MSBuild has two distinct phases:
- **Evaluation** — parses all imports, expands properties/items, evaluates globs. This runs on *every* project load (IDE, CLI, design-time builds). Expensive work here has an outsized impact.
- **Execution** — runs targets and tasks. Only happens during actual builds.

All three issues above put expensive work in the evaluation phase where it's paid repeatedly. Moving work to execution (targets) and constraining globs are the highest-impact fixes.
